read_calcmethod: SMD (Hedges' g) based on change score means and change score sds
justification: Cochrane Handbook recommendations ("conservative correlation of 0.5 was assumed to minimize the likelihood of overstating the intervention effect"), Lakens 2013 for SMD with Hedges' g
imbalance: half of the studies are non-randomized. The RoB checklist (Suppl 3) is not really suitable as its items are for RCTs. The RoB results are therefore questionable.
read_comments: The authors present the imputation of the pre-post correlation as a limitation. Interestingly, the methods section seem to be copy-pasted and included a wording not relevant to the study (stretching vs. control).
recalc_study: Almquist 2022 (Group 2) (41 of 68 in Sup Fig 1, but there might be some double-counting in the forest plot)
recalc_calcmethod: This one is tricky. From how I understand it the authors did in fact not perform any group comparison, but only calculated within changes for each group. They used SMD with Hedges' g correction for this (I could reproduce this with the number they give), but with treating the pre- and post-measures as independent. Thus not considering the pre-post correlation at all despite stating.
recalc_comments:
I also couldn't reproduce their data extraction. The numbers reported in the MA do neither exactly match the data from the plot or table of the original publication (which differ, by the way). So there may be several issues here. 

The main take-away is that the meta-analysis did not really use a group comparison at all despite stating this. Maybe Tab 2 indicates a potential between group comparison, but this rather looks like a comparison of different overall within-groups effects. Maybe the multi-level MA offers any additional information, but I can't find anything more on it. 

So I could reproduce the ES size measure, but not with the method stated. Also I could not reproduce the data extraction.
error_extraction_note: Could not reproduce data extraction for some values
error_reporting_note: The MA reported to use between study SMD with an imputed r of 0.5. Instead they only calculated within study ES, without using any correlation measures.

read_calcmethod: SMD with Hedges' correction of change scores standardized by pre score SD (Morris ppc1, either with Morris or Becker variance estimator)
read_imputemethod: r = 0.7 if not possible to recalculate
justification: all articles included pre and post-case control designs (Becker, 1988 & Morris, 2008)
imbalance: Risk of Bias analysis for randomised trials (RoB2) showed some concerns (64%) and 36% with high risk and analysis for nonrandomized trials (ROBINS-I) showed at least serious risk for each article.
read_comments: the presented formula shows the Morris ppc1 estimate and variance estimator, but also reference to Becker. In general, very detailed explanation of the effect size calculation methods including formulas for point and variance estimate. Sensitivity analysis were performed for r = 0.1, 0.2, ..., 0.9. Data and code available!
recalc_study: Simao, R. (Reference in forest plot wrong. 76 instead of 77), no. 20 in Figure 4
recalc_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction) of change score with raw (pre-) score standardization (Morris ppc1 with Becker variance estimator)
recalc_comments: MA used a different variance estimator than the one given in the article as a formula. I could perfectly recalculate the ES point estimate, but not the forest plot CI, because the aggregation procedure of the 3-level MA seems to change the variance estimates. But using the script, I could fully reproduce the originally calculated ES variance.
error_reporting_note: incorrect formula for variance estimator reported

read_calcmethod: SMD of change score means devided by pooled change score sds. Unclear if Hedges' correction. Indirect link to Borenstein & Hedges for the variance estimator, which would mean that Hedges' correction applies (though their reference makes a mistake in copying the formula).
justification: commonly used, "balancing the risk of over- or underestimating the variance." Reference to Rosenthal
imbalance: only randomized or crossover studies included
recalc_study: Franchi et al (14 of 35 in Fig 2, though there is potentially double-counting of studies)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD of change scores standardized by the change score standard deviation, without Hedges' g correction.
recalc_comments:
I had to try to recalculate three studies: The first and second study presented percentage differences with a measure of variability, so that no correlation imputation was needed. For the third study I could confirm the imputation procedure. In general I could reproduce the effect sizes (when not using Hedges' correct, the correction was not written in the manuscript but may have been implied by the references). I could not reproduce the CIs, maybe in part because I had to extract these values from a low quality figure. 

Also, for the first study I tried to recalculate, the MA authors mistook SEM for SD, making the effect estimate incorrect. In this case the CIs were also definitely off.

[EDIT AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT:]
The authors provided a data sheet with the exact effect sizes and standard errors. With this information, I could recalculated all three effect sizes I tried, after correcting two minor data extraction error from my side and after considering the already described SD/SE mixup for one study. I have updated the R code.

recalc_comments:
Difficult one. By taking the mean and sd values from the forest plot I could fully reproduce the ES + CI. But I could not reproduce the mean and sd in the forest plot. This may be in part because a composite score of different variables has to be calculated for this. But I tried a lot (for two studies) and could barely recalculate one mean values (from 4) and no SD. So I could also not determine the imputation method.

[EDIT AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT:]
The author provided an example of the calculation method, but only referred to the Cochrane handbook when asked for the specific imputation method used. More specifically, he stated that the correlation was imputed using a formula that allows back-calculation from the SD of change-score, though the SD of change scores was the target quantity that needed to be determined. Despite further asking for clarification, I did not get any information on the imputation procedure used. Also the apparent discrepancies between recalculated means and data published in the forest plot was acknowledged by the author, and attributed to the fact, that the MA authors received raw data for some studies, which can lead to different analysis results.

recalc_calcmethod: SMD (Hedges' g) based on change scores and their standard deviations, with RevMan variance estimator
recalc_comments:
I could recalculate the ES and its CI, but only when ignoring different issues I regard as errors. By luck, this study was a special case indicated in the MA text, because it did not present post score sds. Regardless of this I could do the recalculation and found the following issues:
1. The percentage changes are taking as the change scores. This is problematic, because for their sds, the absolute scale is used
2. I think one percentage was incorrectly extracted (18% instead of 19%)
3. The group assignment of percentage changes is incorrect, so the effect is in the wrong direction.
The ES is reproducible when considering these errors, but it is wrong in its magnitude and direction (only its precision is correct). [EDIT AFTER REANALYSIS: I could verify the effect size procedure and get the exact method (eith Hedges' correction, RevMan variance estimator) by recalculating further ES. I also found out that the actual imputed correlation is 0.84 and the the stated 0.5]
error_extraction_note: wrong effect direction; percentages used as absolute values; incorrect digit in mean
error_reporting_note: A correlation of 0.84 was imputed instead of the reported 0.5

read_calcmethod: SMD (Hedges' g), more details are available in Sup. 9. Unclear if change or post-score based, but definitely a form of within group analysis (due to the crossover design). Imputation is based on average correlations for the same outcome, or if not available r = 0.5.
justification: imputation based on previous published (and unpublished) data, link to Cochrane handbook for ES calculation
imbalance: only RCTs included, ROB-2 for bias assessment
read_comments:
Suppl S9 is very detailed. The procedures follow the standard for crossover (so within group analysis), but some formula are confusing. For example the hedges ES is actually not corrected, but simple Cohen's d. 
The analyzed studies are cross-over trial, which influences the choice of ES method.
The study has published raw data and code, which should help during the recalculation.
recalc_study: Carter et al. 2018 (1 out of 7 from Fig S1), condition 2x8min
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on within-group comparisons (with [possibly incorrect] bias corrected variance but not ES). Correlation imputation based on average values for all included studies (though study level results were available).
recalc_comments:
Using the published data and code I could recalculate the ES with CI (otherwise it would be challenging even with the detailed Sup S9). Some things were noteworthy:
1. The MA uses imputed mean correlations for calculating the study level effect size, even if the study has correlation data available
2. within-condition pre-post correlations are computed, but in the analysis they are treated as between-condition post correlations.
3. There was a small data extraction error for the SD_change, extracting 1.6 instead of 1.8. This changed the imputed correlation, but had no impact on the result, because:
4. There was an error in the Excel reference for the recalculated study, using the mean correlation for the wrong outcome measure for the variance calculation (for 'Cerebrovascular conductance' instead of 'Cerebral blood flow velocity', which is 0.69 instead of 0.72)
5. For the ES point estimate, the data sheet and MA report to use Hedges' g, but it is incorrectly calculated as a simple SMD without correction using the pooled sd as standardizer. So in fact this is Cohen's d and for equal n the exact same value as the ES the data sheet reports as uncorrected (which uses average sd instead of pooled sd)
6. Also the correction procedure for the ES variance does not match the ES variance calculation. The variance calculation assumes a repeated measure design (so df=n-1), but the correction formula uses df=n1+n2-2).

So there are a lot of small issues going on here. And most of them could only be identified because the raw data and code was available.

I tried to recalculate the study level effect size by using the correct (mean) study correlation and fully using the appropriate Hedges' correction (so in fact trying to fix the points (1,3,5,6) above:
old ES: 0.32 [-0.08, 0.71]
new ES: 0.29 [-0.17, 0.77]

So in this particular single-study case, it does not make a substantial difference.

[modification after validation: fixed an error in the recalculation script; updated new ES accordingly]
error_calculation_note: between-condition correlation estimated from pre-post correlations; incorrect correlation imputed (Excel reference error); incorrect df for Hedges' correction; missing Hedges' correction for point ES

recalc_comments:
okay, difficult. First, because the MA forest plot does not report numbers, so individual study effects have to be extracted from the plot. Second, because all original research I reviewer also did not report numbers, but only figures, so again data has to be extracted from plots.

Overall I tried 3 studies (and skipped another). (1) was Gorman & Ferron, I could only nearly match the ES and the CI, but there were also missing data and inconsistencies in the original work. I skipped the next one because the MA states it used two outcomes from it without telling how they were combined. (2) was Smeeton where I may have gotten somewhere in the range of the target ES, but couldn't continue because the original data figure was missing clear information. (3) Murgia, I could get near the target ES and CI using the methods described in the MA text, so given the inaccuracies of two plot extraction processes here, this could maybe be considered a successful reproduction.

[Adjustments after validation stage: Fixed an error in (1) reproduction that lead to incorrect ES, calculated CI in (3). Overall I still did not get perfect matches, but changed my overall judgement from no reproduction to near reproduction]

recalc_study: Spillane et al. 2009 (12 out of 12 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: MD (not assuming homoscedasticity)
recalc_imputemethod: 0.975 (in this instance, so exactly as the authors described it)
recalc_comments: I could fully reproduce the ES, but only when I multiplied the control group sample size, mean, and sd by 1/2. I think this was done because there are two intervention group for this particular study (though not in the same forest plot) and the MA authors used this to account for potential double-counting). But then one should halve the sample size and definitely not also the mean and sd. This can (and in this instance does) highly bias ES towards exaggerating treatment effects. I found this out by looking at the data sheet the MA authors provided on the OSF, so providing data and code here proved to be extremely helpful. (On another note, the reference number was incorrect in the forest plot)
error_calculation_note: to account for multiple inclusion of control groups, the authors multiplied not only the sample size x0.5, but also the mean and the standard deviation

read_calcmethod: SMD of change scores (and their sds)
read_imputemethod: r = 0.5 if no change score sd was reported
justification: SMD because outcome scales slightly differed. For the correlations, Higgins et al. 2003 is referenced, but the article does not deal with the topic. The two values 0.5 and 0.7 are used with reference to Rosenthal (r=0.7) and Follmann (r=0.5). The MA states that in case they were "similar", r = 0.5 was chosen for the MA, and from Tab 1 it seems that in fact r = 0.5 was always used.

I could not exactly match the extracted numbers underlying the ES, probably because the MA authors had access to the raw data (from the same research group) and took values without rounding. When taking these values, I could reproduce the ES. (I also checked for another study that values were correctly extracted). I did not check the correlation imputation, as it was not necessary for the studies I looked at. The MA states it was actually only necessary for one study, but its Table 1 suggests that imputation was done for 3 studies. I looked at them but the data extraction seems non-trivial, so I did not formally test the imputation procedure. Also, the reference number in the forest plot was incorrect.

status_explanation: analysis does not match inclusion criteria (no formal group comparison)
read_comments:
This study does not formally have a group comparison. Analyses are performed within groups, including studies that had no comparison group. So the research design may not fully correspond to what we are looking for. There are two possible ways to deal with this: 
(1) exclude the study because it does not exactly match the design we are looking for
(2) include the study because the abstract suggests a design that does match our criteria and only the analysis method was not corresponding and should be treated as a special case.

this is an umbrella review, but has a dedicated MA of RCTs. The effect size calculation method is very confusing: "The absolute change in mean difference and standard deviation
of the outcome value from postintervention between groups (interval training versus MICT/CON) in each study was calculated". This could be both change-score based or post-score based. Maybe also change score with post score sd standardization?

The methods section presents an SMD formula that is very basic. An interesting find is that the CIs are reportedly based on a t-distribution (instead of the typical z-distribution)

[EDIT AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT: The authors declined to share data, but provided an analysis script with additional explanation, which helped for the recalculation]
recalc_study: Berton et al 2022 (3 out of 24 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: First, calculation of separate within-group SMDs standardized by the change score sd and using a specific variance estimator (the metafor::escalc(method = "SMCC") defaults). Second, subtracting the point effect estimates and adding the variances.
recalc_comments:
Frankly, I have no idea how the ES were calculated. The confidence intervals seem way to small. I tried change-score and post score based methods for three examples. Post-score seems more promising to me, but again I could not recalculate the ES.

[EDIT AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT: Using the script provided the authors I could reach a successful reproduction. The authors first calculate within-group ES and then combine them. However there is a mixing up of the standard error and the variance of the ES. The authors took the variance of the ES as its standard error in the analysis (although this issue is not present in the analysis script provided, it appears to be present in the analysis, given the published forest plots). This leads to far too narrow confidence intervals for all ES. Another finding is that a z-distribution was used to calculate the confidence intervals instead of the t-distribution that was mentioned in the article. I have uploaded an updated recalculation script]
error_calculation_note: mixup between ES SE and var: the authors used the SE(ES) in their MA code although they would have needed to use the var(ES); incorrect combination of within group ES
error_reporting_note: used CI based on z-distribution instead on the reported t-distribution

recalc_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction) of post scores for repeated measurements (but using the d_repeated measures method, which has the sd of difference scores as the denominator and the standardizer 2*sqrt(1-r)). Possibly r = 0.5 for the repeated measures correlation, CI calculation unclear.
recalc_comments:
I could reproduce the ES points estimates when using the method described above. This is only valid because the study designs were all cross-over. I am quite sure that r is equal to or near 0.5 but cannot validate this, as I could not fully reproduce the CIs. When I use the SE given in the forest plot I could reproduce the CI given in the forest plot. But I don't know how to get the SE in the forest plot from the results of the online calculator the MA authors used.

[UPDATE AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT:]
The authors provided verification that they indeed used the online calculator with the specified parameters. Upon reanalysis, I found that when using the rounded SE, back-calculated from the CI given in the online calculator, this matches the SE in the forest plot. I tried to recalculate the CI in the online calculator, I got very near when using a t-distribution based CI of a SMCR with an imputed r = 0.5. I have updated the R code.
error_calculation_note: Hedges' correction only applied to point ES but not to variance

read_comments:
This is a Bayesian Model Based Network MA with time-course and dose-response analysis. The text presents how ES were calculated, but no individual ES are shown in the article or supplemental information, so no recalculation can be performed. The public repository that should contain data and code for the study is empty.

[EDIT AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT: After writing the corresponding author, the authors updated their study repository to include data and code. The data and code shows that no pairwise analyses were performed and that effect sizes were only calculated within individual treatment groups but not studies (so no direct group comparisons were performed). (Also the within group effect sized were calculated as if they were between group comparisons, but this is not a matter here). So I would argue to exclude this MA, as it does not match the analysis design we are looking for.]

justification: Lakens 2013 for calculating the within group ES and using the Hedges' correction, Kebede et al. 2019 for calculating the correlation from reported sds, Madeyski & Kitchenham 2018 for the between group SMD, Goulet-Pelletier & Cousineau (2018) for the variance estimator
imbalance: only crossover trials included, self-developed checklist for bias-assessment
read_comments: The procedure is well-explained, well-referenced, and the appendix provides all formula used. I am unsure whether the used approach is mathematically correct, because (1) the variance estimator is for independent groups but also used for the within group ES here (2) there is an error (typo?) in the formula for calculating the pre-post correlation (3) the practice of standardizing a difference of standardized means may yield results that are different to interpret (4) The cross-over nature of the trials is not fully taken into account (e.g., not using the between condition correlation).
recalc_study: Jones et al. 2008 (9 out of 11 from Fig 2A)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on within-group SMD (both with Hedges' correction and both with an independent group variance estimator).
recalc_comments:
I could finally perfectly recalculate a given effect size, but only when following the mistakes from the MA analysis: 
1. the formula for calculating the pre-post correlation is not just incorrectly applied but also contains an error, with the result that a correlation of r = 0.5 is always imputed
2. the variance formula (which is inappropriate for the within group design) also has an error in the calculation of the degrees of freedom, as has the Hedges' correction factor
3. The standardization procedure of the between group ES standardizes already standardized SMDs by raw score SDs, which will almost always leads to extremely small ES (but large CIs)
4. The between group ES does not take the crossover design into account but treats the am and pm groups as independent (loss of power)
error_calculation_note: several statistically incorrect formulas are used, leading to a double standardization of the ES, which makes all effect very small (and CIs large)

recalc_comments: I could perfectly recalculate the ES and CI for 2 out of 3 cases when using a uncorrected SMD with raw (post!) score standardization. This means that the references paper Morris 2008 was not followed, since it recommends the Hedges' correction and pre-score sd standardization. It also means that the assumed correlation of r = 0.7 has no effect on the calculated ES, since no correlation is needed for the calculation. This is a bit strange since the correlation imputation was stated several times in the MA. But I could perfectly recalculate two out of three ES with CI with my method (accuracy of <0.001), and the third was only off by 0.01. Incorrect reference numbers in forest plot

[EDIT AFTER REANALYSIS:] I was unable to determine whether Hedges' correction was applied, but because of the large sample sizes this does not make any difference here (because of the labeling of the CMA-based forest plot I assume that no correction was applied).

read_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction)
justification: none
imbalance: only RCTs included, SIGN-RCT checklist for bias assessment
read_comments: some crossover studies were included following the guidelines by Elbourne et al. 2002. Whether change or post scores were used is unclear from the text.

recalc_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction) of post scores and their sd (with Borenstein variance estimator)
recalc_comments: I couldn't match the ES of the first study (the study only reported change scores and no post scores), but the recalculation worked for the second (which I upload here). The MA authors use the Borenstein variance estimator, despite stating that they used metafor for the MA. This is rather uncommon, because that estimator is not the default setting in metafor. Maybe the ES calculation was done with another software.

imbalance: RCT and non-RCTs included, modified Downs & Black checklist for bias assessment
read_comments: The exact way to calculate the standardizer for the ES is unclear to me, as is the var estimator, which is not reported.

recalc_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges correction) of change scores standardized by the the averages of the pre and post sd (with Borenstein variance estimator)

read_calcmethod: SMD, possibly based on change scores
justification: none
imbalance: randomized and non-randomized studies included, modified Downs & Black checklist for bias assessment
read_comments: From the tables, figures and text I assume that a change score based approach was used, but this is not really clear
recalc_study: Irving et al. 2015 (18 out of 21 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD based on change scores and their SD
recalc_comments: The study I used for the reanalysis did only present pre- and percentage change data. These data were used to calculate post score, with the percentage change score SD taken as the percentage post score sd (which is statistically incorrect). Then pre and post scores were used (with imputed correlation) to calculate change scores. I would not have been able to reproduce this, if the authors did not provide their extracted data, and their input data for the ES calculation in a table and the forest plot, so this was a great help. In the forest plot, the two groups of the same study were mixed up.
error_extraction_note: percentage change score sd taken as percentage post score sd

justification: MD if same scale of outcome measures, SMD if different scales were used.

recalc_study: Bjerre et al. 2019 (1 out of 2 from Fig 2) and Sundstrup et al. 2016 (2 out of 4 from Fig 4)
recalc_calcmethod: MD of change scores and change score SD (with homogenouos variances) or SMD (with Hedges' correction) of change scores with post score SD standardization (using Borenstein variance estimator)

I could reproduce the two SMDs I tried (one with very minor rounding error). [EDIT AFTER REANALYSIS: I could also recalculate the MD when using the change score SDs for the variance calculation, which is different from the SMD which uses post scores. I will here select the SMD as the target ES for recalculation, as it is more prominent than the MD in the results section of the abstract]

read_calcmethod: SMD (without Hedges' correction) based on change scores and their SD (with metafor variance estimator)
justification: Borenstein for using SMD, Dankel & Loenneke 2021 for standardizing by the change score SD instead of the pre score SD, Abe et al. 2000 as a primary study for using 0.923 as a correlation imputation for leg extension, different other primary studies for provided a range of correlations (0.85-0.99) to justify the used r = 0.9.
imbalance: probably randomized and non-randomized controlled studies included, ROB-2 for bias assessment
read_comments: Very detailed account of the effect size calculation including formulas for the point and variance estimates and references for the used procedures and imputed values. Sensitivity analyses for r = 0.7, 0.8 were performed.
recalc_study: Botton et al. 2016 (19 out of 24 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD (without Hedges' correction) based on change scores and their SD
recalc_comments: I succeeded with the third ES to recalculate, the first probably had a slight differences in the CI, the second probably mistakenly took the unstandardized mean difference as the point estimate (and had the wrong effect direction, though the ES was very small), the third worked perfectly. Notably, although the MA stated that the used a particular r instead of the standard r=0.9 for leg extension measurements, they used r = 0.9 also for leg extension.
error_extraction_note: MD instead of SMD for point estimate, incorrect effect direction; unclear for another effect size
error_reporting_note: correlation of 0.9 was used for all measures (instead of 0.923 which was reported for own particular outcome)

read_calcmethod: SMD (possibly change score based with change score standardization)
justification: imputed correlation is based on an earlier MA on the same topic (Grgic et al. 2022), that used the correlation as a conservative estimate based on original studies that allowed to recalculate the correlation.
imbalance: only randomized trials included, TESTEX scale for bias assessment
read_comments: sensitivity analyses were performed for correlation ranging from 0.6-0.9; open data and code available. The the Supplementary Materials show and the limitations section discussed that slightly lower correlations (r<0.74) lead to non-significant pooled effects
recalc_study: Terada et al. 2022 (18 out of 18 from Fig 2)
recalc_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction) of change scores and their SD (with metafor variance estimator)
recalc_comments: One minor data extraction error occurred for the recalculated ES, which only lead to a very minor difference of 0.01 for the ES point estimate. I could verify the data extraction error with the help of the provided raw data.

read_calcmethod: SMD based on post scores
justification: none

read_comments: The MA included both crossover and parallel group trials. For crossover trials the ES is calculated as a within-group ES, with r = 0.7 (reference to Rosenthal 1991 and an earlier MA using 0.7 [Kishita et al. 2017, who also reference Rosenthal 1993]), with sensitivity analyses setting r = 0.5, 0.8

Also the first recalculated ES had the wrong direction of the effect (although the ES was small, so this has almost no impact). The second recalculation worked well when using rounded values.

read_calcmethod: SMD (with Hedges' correction)
justification: reference to Hedges & Olkin 1985 for bias correction
imbalance: only RCTs included, PEDro scale for bias assessment
read_comments: Possible change scores were used, but this is not clear from the text. Open data and open code.
recalc_study: Campos et al. 2013 (7 out of 19 from Fig 3)
recalc_calcmethod: the difference of the separately calculated pre- and post-SMD as the ES point estimate, with the post-SMD variance (using RevMan variance estimator).
recalc_comments:
I could recalculate one ES using the method describe above (but in this case, the MA authors took the SE for the SD). But for the first and third ES, I could absolutely not recalculate the values, although the data was sufficient and matches the published MA data. The published data and code does not provide any information on how the ES were calculated. There may be an important undocumented step in the ES calculation, that was by chance not relevant to the one ES for which the recalculation succeeded.

[UPDATE AFTER AUTHOR CONTACT:]
The authors provided a spreadsheet with the calculation. However, approximately half of the effect sizes did not match the published forest plot data, which the authors stated was an error they cannot explain. They have contacted the journal for correction. When using the correct ES, I could recalculate the ES and CI using the rather unusual procedure using separate SMD for pre- and post values. I have updated the code and the primary article reanalyzed to Holviala et al.
error_extraction_note: half of the effect sizes do not match the raw data, unclear why (even to the authors)
error_calculation_note: incorrect calculation procedure (difference of seperate pre-SMD and post-SMD, variance only for post-SMD)
